document_text:
1
AI-Enhanced Spatial Cellular Traffic Demand
Prediction with Contextual Clustering and Error
Correction for 5G/6G Planning
Mohamad Alkadamani, Colin Brown, and Halim Yanikomeroglu
Abstract—Accurate spatial prediction of cellular traffic de-
mand is essential for 5G NR capacity planning, network densifi-
cation, and data-driven 6G planning. Although machine learning
can fuse heterogeneous geospatial and socio-economic layers to
estimate fine-grained demand maps, spatial autocorrelation can
cause neighborhood leakage under naive train/test splits, inflating
accuracy and weakening planning reliability. This paper presents
an AI-driven framework that reduces leakage and improves
spatial generalization via a context-aware two-stage splitting
strategy with residual spatial error correction. Experiments using
crowdsourced usage indicators across five major Canadian cities
show consistent mean absolute error (MAE) reductions relative
to location-only clustering, supporting more reliable bandwidth
provisioning and evidence-based spectrum planning and sharing
assessments.
Index Terms—5G NR, 6G, cellular traffic demand predic-
tion, spectrum planning, spatial autocorrelation, spatial cross-
validation
I. INTRODUCTION
A key capability for AI-enabled wireless networks and
cognitive spectrum management is characterizing how cellular
traffic demand varies across space, particularly in dense urban
regions. For 5G and beyond, spatial demand hotspots influence
carrier bandwidth selection, small-cell deployment, capacity
expansion, and the feasibility of spectrum access mechanisms.
Spatial demand prediction can also help regulators and plan-
ners identify regions at risk of under- or over-provisioning and
screen spectrum-sharing feasibility in longer-horizon planning.
Estimating cellular traffic demand, especially for commer-
cial mobile applications, is complex and shaped by inter-
related factors such as technological evolution, regulations,
usage trends, and socio-economic conditions. Traditionally,
planning studies relied on expert assessments in industry
whitepapers [1] or simplified models [2]. Increasing system
complexity and data availability have accelerated interest in
data-driven planning and resource-allocation methods [3]–
[5]. These approaches, including ML and advanced analytics,
can infer spatial demand distributions and support planning
decisions via predictive traffic-demand maps [6].
Data-driven spatial traffic-demand prediction faces chal-
lenges specific to geospatial wireless data, including hetero-
geneous feature resolutions, high dimensionality, and the need
M. Alkadamani is with Innovation, Science and Economic Develop-
ment Canada (ISED) and Carleton University, Ottawa, Canada (e-mail:
mohamad.alkadamani@ised-isde.gc.ca).
C. Brown is with the Communications Research Centre (CRC), Ottawa,
Ontario, Canada.
H. Yanikomeroglu is with Carleton University, Ottawa, Ontario, Canada.to handle spatial autocorrelation to avoid biased evaluation. A
central concern isspatial information leakage: nearby samples
are statistically dependent, so naive train/test splitting can yield
over-optimistic accuracy and misleading generalization claims.
Prior work on traffic-demand proxy modeling provides a
starting point. In [7], diverse geospatial inputs were studied
alongside a demand proxy, and location-based clustering was
used to improve train/test independence; [6] advanced the
proxy and interpretability aspects. Spatio-temporal traffic pre-
diction has also been studied using recurrent neural networks
with geo-referenced traffic measurements to capture temporal
dynamics and geographic variability [8]. From the leakage-
mitigation perspective, spatial cross-validation techniques are
surveyed in [9], [10], and related fields propose cluster-based
and hybrid splitting strategies [11], [12]. However, many
clustering-based splits remaincontext-blind, relying mainly on
location (or generic clustering) without explicitly enforcing
representativeness in land-use and functional context; conse-
quently, dependence can still leak across folds and evaluation
may remain optimistic.
This paper proposes a context-aware framework for spatial
cellular traffic-demand prediction that improves spatial gen-
eralization under spatial autocorrelation and supports reliable
5G/6G planning. Contributions include a context-aware two-
stage splitting strategy that combines spatial clustering with
land-use/context clustering to form representative folds and
reduce leakage relative to location-only clustering, and a five-
city Canadian evaluation with planning-oriented mappings
that translate prediction error into bandwidth-dimensioning
error and congestion-risk screening for carrier bandwidth and
spectrum-provisioning assessments.
The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. Sec-
tion II describes the data model and feature mapping. Sec-
tion III characterizes spatial dependency using Moran’s I.
Section IV presents the proposed two-stage splitting strategy.
Section V reports performance results, Section VI links errors
to 5G/6G planning metrics, and Section VII concludes.
II. DATA-DRIVENSPATIALTRAFFICDEMANDMODELING
Spatial traffic-demand prediction is formulated as super-
vised learning over heterogeneous geospatial features by map-
ping both feature layers and a traffic-demand proxy to a
common geographic unit.
A. Grid-cell representation and study areas
The study region is partitioned into uniform squaregrid
cellsof approximately1.5km×1.5km. The grid cell is anarXiv:2603.10800v1  [cs.LG]  11 Mar 2026

2
analysis unit used to align datasets to a common spatial
resolution. Five major Canadian cities are considered: Mon-
treal, Vancouver, Greater Toronto, Ottawa, and Calgary. Let
S={s 1, . . . , s n}denote the set ofngrid cells. Eachs ihas a
feature vectorx i∈Rmand a targety irepresenting a traffic-
demand proxy, with learning objectiveˆy i=fθ(xi).
B. Traffic demand proxy from crowdsourced measurements
Direct traffic measurements are typically restricted to mobile
network operators and are unavailable for public studies. A
traffic-demand proxyy iis therefore derived from crowd-
sourced mobile usage indicators collected via application-
embedded SDKs and aggregated to the grid-cell level. The
dataset comprises approximately 15 million measurements
across the five cities over one month in 2023.
In the 4G/5G transition regime, the indicators reflect com-
bined activity and represent total traffic intensity rather than
generation-specific load. The proxy increases with the number
and persistence of observed user connections within a grid
cell and, although bytes transferred are not explicitly encoded,
captures the dominant busy-hour spatial structure (dense con-
nections imply higher load, while sparse activity implies lower
demand). After filtering and aggregation, each grid cells iis
assignedy ipaired withx ifor supervised learning.
C. Feature layers and grid-cell mapping
Input features originate from heterogeneous sources and are
standardized to the grid-cell representation. Feature layers may
be defined on administrative units (e.g., census subdivisions
and dissemination areas), as points (e.g., points of interest
and businesses), or as polygons (e.g., land-use/land-cover).
Geometry-aware mapping assigns each grid cells ia consistent
feature vectorx i.
Feature layers are temporally aligned to overlap with the
same one-month window of the crowdsourced dataset; some
layers have coarser temporal granularity than others but still
overlap the analysis period. For areal datasets, grid-cell values
are obtained by spatial overlay using area-weighted allocation
from source polygons to intersecting grid cells. For categorical
polygon layers (e.g., land-use), the dominant class within each
grid cell is used; point-based layers are mapped via counts or
density estimates (normalized by cell area).
Mapped features include socio-economic variables (popula-
tion density and related census indicators), urban infrastructure
(counts/densities of businesses, roads, and points of interest),
land-use type (land-use/land-cover attributes), and network
infrastructure (an indicator of cellular infrastructure presence).
All layers are aligned to the grid and combined intox i,
yielding{(x i, yi)}n
i=1. Figure 1 summarizes the pipeline.
III. SPATIALDEPENDENCYCHARACTERIZATION
Urban traffic-demand maps exhibit spatial dependence:
neighboring areas often share land use, socio-economic condi-
tions, and mobility-driven usage, producing correlated demand
values. Quantifying spatial dependence (i) motivates leakage-
aware splitting and (ii) informs the spatial scale required
to separate neighborhoods and obtain realistic generalization
estimates.
Fig. 1. Modeling pipeline.
Fig. 2. Moran’s I versus distance (grid cells).
A. Global Moran’s I for spatial autocorrelation
Global Moran’s I quantifies city-wide spatial autocorrelation

B. Local Moran’s I for spatial clusters and outliers
Local Moran’s I localizes spatial clusters and outliers. For
grid cells i,

wherew ijdefines the neighborhood.
Local Moran’s I yields four spatial association types: High-
High (HH), highy iwith high neighbors; Low-Low (LL), low
yiwith low neighbors; High-Low (HL), highy iwith low

3
Fig. 3. Local Moran’s I clusters across five Canadian cities.
neighbors; and Low-High (LH), lowy iwith high neighbors.
Figure 3 visualizes these associations, with dark red indicating
HH clusters (persistent hotspots) and light red indicating LL
clusters (persistent low-demand regions). Transitional areas
often exhibit HL/LH behavior and can be challenging for
generalization when neighbors are split across folds.
IV. TWO-STAGEDATASPLITTING ANDSPATIALERROR
CORRECTION
Spatial autocorrelation makes random splitting unreliable
because adjacent grid cells can share near-duplicate context,
inflating validation accuracy when neighbors fall in differ-
ent folds. Location-only clustering reduces leakage but can
produce context-imbalanced folds (e.g., commercial versus
residential); the proposed two-stage procedure enforces spatial
separation and context diversity and is followed by spatial
error correction as shown in Fig. 4.
A. Stage 1: spatial clustering for leakage reduction
Stage 1 partitions the study area into spatially cohesive
blocks by applying k-Means to grid-cell centroids
min
{Ci}k
i=1kX
i=1X
sj∈Ci∥pj−µi∥2,(3)
wherep jis the coordinate vector ofs j,Ciis theith spatial
cluster, andµiis its centroid. The parameterkcontrols cluster
diameter and the extent to which correlated neighbors are
separated across folds; Fig. 2 guides selection by indicating
the dominant correlation range (up torgrid cells) that most
train/validation boundaries should exceed.
B. Stage 2: context-aware refinement within spatial clusters
Stage 2 refines each spatial cluster using context features
(e.g., land-use/land-cover and related attributes) to improve
representativeness in feature space and avoid folds dominated
by a single context type. A normalized dissimilarity between
saands bwithin the same spatial cluster is
d(sa, sb) =mX
ℓ=1|xaℓ−xbℓ|
σℓ,(4)whereσ ℓis the standard deviation of featureℓ. Categorical
land-use can be represented via one-hot encoding so that
dissimilarity reflects context changes.
Stage 2 produces sub-clusters that remain spatially con-
tained within Stage-1 blocks and are more homogeneous in
context. Sub-clusters are assigned to folds to preserve Stage-1
spatial separation while ensuring each fold contains a mixture
of contexts. Figure 5 illustrates the effect in Montreal. As
shown, the location-only k-Means partition uses five folds,
whereas the two-stage procedure yields three folds because
refinement and fold construction merge sub-clusters into the
smallest set of folds that remains spatially separated and
sufficiently distinct in land-use context.
C. Learning model and spatial error correction
XGBoost is used as the base predictor due to strong
performance on structured tabular features and nonlinear in-
teractions. Letˆy idenote the prediction ande i=yi−ˆyithe
residual. Even under leakage-reduced splitting, residuals can
remain spatially correlated due to unmodeled neighborhood
effects and latent variables, creating geographically coherent
bias. A Spatial Error Model (SEM) is applied to the residual
process
y=Xβ+ϵ,ϵ=λWϵ+u,(5)
whereWis a spatial weights matrix (distance threshold ork-
nearest neighbors),λcaptures residual spatial dependence, and
uis i.i.d. noise. The SEM represents residuals as a spatially
filtered process.
SEM refinement is implemented as post-processing: XG-
Boost is trained on two-stage folds, residuals are computed
on training data,λis estimated givenW, and the spatial filter
is applied to correct predictions. A regularized objective that
penalizes spatially structured residuals is
L(θ, λ) =1

whereθdenotes XGBoost parameters. The third term discour-
ages residual autocorrelation, improving robustness in unseen
geographic regions.
V. PERFORMANCEEVALUATION
This section evaluates predictive performance and spatial
generalization under the proposed splitting strategy using
MAE andR2, with learning curves assessing leakage-driven
overfitting under spatially structured validation.
A. Cross-city evaluation
Table I reports two evaluations. A leave-one-city-out config-
uration assesses transferability by holding each city out as an
unseen test set while training on the remaining cities. An “All
Cities” configuration performs splits across the pooled dataset
to reflect within-distribution performance.
Across both configurations, two-stage splitting consistently
reduces MAE relative to location-only clustering, indicating
that context-aware folds better reflect deployment hetero-
geneity. SEM provides additional MAE reductions, consistent
with residual spatial structure not fully captured by the base

4
Fig. 4. Two-stage clustering and spatial error correction framework.
Fig. 5. Comparison of clustering techniques for Montreal.
predictor. AlthoughR2gains are smaller, the systematic MAE
reduction improves absolute demand estimates, which is the
critical quantity for downstream planning.

B. Learning curves
Figure 6 compares learning curves for location-only cluster-
ing, two-stage spatial+context splitting, and two-stage splitting
with SEM refinement. The training–validation gap indicates
overfitting under the chosen split, and shaded bands reflect
fold variability.
Location-only clustering shows a large training–validation
gap, indicating limited transfer to spatially distinct regions.
Two-stage splitting reduces this gap and improves validation
MAE, while SEM refinement further lowers validation error
by correcting residual spatial dependence.
VI. WIRELESSPLANNINGLINK FOR5G/6G
This section presents a 5G NR mid-band (3.5 GHz) case
study mapping the prediction errors in Section V to bandwidth-
Fig. 6. Learning curves comparison for different clustering strategies.
dimensioning and congestion-risk metrics.
A. Bandwidth dimensioning
Offered downlink traffic demand (bps) in grid cells iis
mapped from the proxyy ias
Di=κy i,(7)
whereκis the busy-hour traffic per proxy unit;κ= 50kbps

Letγ idenote the downlink SINR random variable over the

Figure 8 comparesP cong(B)under the reference surface
Di(“Observed demand”) and predicted surfaces ˆDi, where
deviations from the reference quantify planning risk (over-
estimation/underestimation of congestion). Leakage-reduced
splitting and SEM refinement improve spatial generalization
and shift the inferred congestion curve toward the observed
curve acrossB. For illustration without operator traffic traces,
Fig. 8 uses an “All Cities” case study combining a heavy-
tailed spatial demand distribution (log-normal proxy field)
with prediction-error models calibrated to the MAE values in
Table I, highlighting how moderate MAE differences translate
into meaningful shifts in estimated congested area.
VII. CONCLUSION
This paper presented an AI-driven framework for spatial cel-
lular traffic-demand prediction to support data-driven 5G/6G
capacity and spectrum planning. The framework addresses
spatial autocorrelation, which can inflate evaluation when
training and validation sets are not properly separated, via a
context-aware two-stage splitting strategy that reduces leakage
while preserving functional representativeness across folds,
and SEM refinement that mitigates residual spatial errors.
Fig. 8. All Cities:P cong (B)versusBunder observed and predicted demand.
Evaluation across five major Canadian cities shows consis-
tent MAE reductions relative to location-only clustering, with
additional gains from SEM correction. A planning-oriented
5G NR mid-band case study demonstrates how the predictive
gains translate into more reliable carrier bandwidth selection
and provisioning assessments while controlling leakage and
geographically coherent bias.

structure.title: AI-Enhanced Spatial Cellular Traffic Demand Prediction with Contextual Clustering and Error Correction for 5G/6G Planning
structure.summary: The partial document discusses AI-enhanced spatial cellular traffic demand prediction for 5G/6G planning. It highlights the importance of accurate spatial prediction of cellular traffic demand for network densification and data-driven planning. The document proposes a context-aware framework that reduces spatial autocorrelation and improves spatial generalization via a two-stage splitting strategy with residual spatial error correction. The main points covered include the challenges of spatial traffic-demand prediction, the limitations of traditional planning methods, and the benefits of a data-driven approach using machine learning and advanced analytics. The document also introduces a context-aware framework that combines spatial clustering with land-use/context clustering to form representative folds and reduce leakage. The study uses crowdsourced usage indicators across five major Canadian cities to evaluate the performance of the proposed framework, with a focus on reliable bandwidth provisioning and evidence-based spectrum planning and sharing assessments.

nodes.title: Abstract
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses AI-enhanced spatial cellular traffic demand prediction for 5G/6G planning. The main points covered include the importance of accurate spatial prediction of cellular traffic demand, the challenges of spatial autocorrelation and information leakage in traditional machine learning approaches, and the proposal of a context-aware framework to improve spatial generalization. The framework uses a two-stage splitting strategy that combines spatial clustering with land-use/context clustering to reduce leakage and improve prediction accuracy. The document also highlights the application of the proposed framework in five major Canadian cities, with results showing consistent mean absolute error reductions relative to location-only clustering, supporting more reliable bandwidth provisioning and evidence-based spectrum planning and sharing assessments.

structure.title: I. INTRODUCTION
structure.summary: The partial document discusses AI-enhanced spatial cellular traffic demand prediction for 5G/6G planning. It highlights the importance of accurate spatial prediction of cellular traffic demand for capacity planning, network densification, and data-driven planning. The document proposes a context-aware framework that reduces leakage and improves spatial generalization via a two-stage splitting strategy with residual spatial error correction. It also characterizes spatial dependency using Moran's I and presents a data-driven spatial traffic demand model using heterogeneous geospatial features and a traffic-demand proxy derived from crowdsourced mobile usage indicators. The main points covered include the challenges of spatial information leakage, the need for context-aware splitting strategies, and the application of spatial cross-validation techniques to improve planning reliability and reduce errors in bandwidth provisioning and spectrum planning.

structure.title: II. DATA-DRIVEN SPATIAL TRAFFIC DEMAND MODELING
structure.summary: The partial document discusses the analysis of traffic demand in five major Canadian cities using a grid-cell representation. The main points covered include the use of crowdsourced mobile usage indicators as a traffic-demand proxy, the creation of feature layers from heterogeneous sources, and the characterization of spatial dependence in urban traffic-demand maps. The document also introduces the concept of Global Moran's I for quantifying city-wide spatial autocorrelation and Local Moran's I for identifying spatial clusters and outliers. The analysis aims to inform the development of a modeling pipeline for traffic demand prediction, with a focus on reducing leakage and improving generalization estimates. Key aspects of the document include data collection, feature engineering, and spatial analysis, with applications in urban planning and transportation management.

nodes.title: A. Grid-cell representation and study areas
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the analysis of traffic demand in five major Canadian cities using a grid-cell representation. The main points covered include the derivation of a traffic-demand proxy from crowdsourced mobile usage indicators, the creation of feature layers from heterogeneous sources, and the characterization of spatial dependence in urban traffic-demand maps. The document also introduces the use of Global Moran's I and Local Moran's I to quantify spatial autocorrelation and identify spatial clusters and outliers. The analysis aims to inform the development of a modeling pipeline for supervised learning, with a focus on reducing leakage and obtaining realistic generalization estimates. Key features considered include socio-economic variables, urban infrastructure, land-use type, and network infrastructure.

nodes.title: B. Traffic demand proxy from crowdsourced measurements
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses the analysis of traffic demand in five major Canadian cities using a grid-cell representation. The main points covered include the derivation of a traffic-demand proxy from crowdsourced mobile usage indicators, the creation of feature layers from heterogeneous sources, and the characterization of spatial dependence in urban traffic-demand maps. The document also introduces the use of Global Moran's I and Local Moran's I to quantify spatial autocorrelation and identify spatial clusters and outliers. The analysis aims to inform the development of a modeling pipeline for supervised learning, with a focus on reducing leakage and obtaining realistic generalization estimates. Key features considered include socio-economic variables, urban infrastructure, land-use type, and network infrastructure.

nodes.title: C. Feature layers and grid-cell mapping
nodes.summary:
The partial document discusses a study on traffic demand prediction in five major Canadian cities. The main points covered include: 
1. The use of crowdsourced mobile usage indicators to derive a traffic-demand proxy, 
2. The creation of a feature vector for each grid cell using heterogeneous sources, 
3. The characterization of spatial dependence in urban traffic-demand maps using Global Moran's I and Local Moran's I, 
4. A two-stage data splitting procedure to reduce leakage and improve spatial generalization, 
5. The use of a Spatial Error Model to correct for residual spatial correlation, and 
6. The evaluation of predictive performance using Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and R-squared (R2) metrics.

structure.title: III. SPATIAL DEPENDENCY CHARACTERIZATION
structure.summary: The partial document discusses a two-stage data splitting procedure for spatial data to reduce leakage and improve spatial generalization in predictive models. The main points covered include the use of Local Moran's I to identify clusters, a two-stage procedure involving spatial clustering and context-aware refinement, and the application of a Spatial Error Model to correct for residual spatial dependence. The document also evaluates the performance of the proposed splitting strategy using metrics such as MAE and R2, and demonstrates its effectiveness in reducing overfitting and improving transferability across different cities. Additionally, the use of XGBoost as a base predictor and the implementation of SEM refinement as post-processing are highlighted as key components of the approach.

nodes.title: A. Global Moran’s I for spatial autocorrelation
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a two-stage data splitting procedure for spatial data to reduce leakage and improve spatial generalization in predictive models. The first stage involves spatial clustering using k-Means to partition the study area into spatially cohesive blocks, while the second stage refines each spatial cluster using context features to improve representativeness and avoid folds dominated by a single context type. The procedure is followed by spatial error correction using a Spatial Error Model (SEM) to account for residual spatial dependence. The document also evaluates the predictive performance of the proposed splitting strategy using metrics such as MAE and R2, and demonstrates its effectiveness in reducing overfitting and improving spatial generalization across different cities. Key points include the use of Local Moran's I clusters to identify persistent hotspots and low-demand regions, the application of XGBoost as the base predictor, and the implementation of SEM refinement as post-processing to correct predictions and improve robustness.

nodes.title: B. Local Moran’s I for spatial clusters and outliers
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a two-stage data splitting procedure for spatial data to reduce leakage and improve spatial generalization in predictive models. The main points covered include the use of Local Moran's I to identify clusters, a two-stage procedure involving spatial clustering and context-aware refinement, and the application of a Spatial Error Model to correct for residual spatial dependence. The document also evaluates the performance of the proposed splitting strategy using metrics such as MAE and R2, and demonstrates its effectiveness in reducing overfitting and improving transferability across different cities. Additionally, the use of XGBoost as a base predictor and the implementation of SEM refinement as post-processing are highlighted as key components of the approach.

structure.title: IV. TWO-STAGE DATA SPLITTING AND SPATIAL ERROR CORRECTION
structure.summary: The partial document discusses a two-stage data splitting procedure for spatial data to reduce leakage and improve spatial generalization in predictive models. The first stage involves spatial clustering using k-Means to partition the study area into spatially cohesive blocks, while the second stage refines these clusters using context features to improve representativeness and avoid folds dominated by a single context type. The procedure is followed by spatial error correction using a Spatial Error Model (SEM) to account for residual spatial dependence. The document also evaluates the performance of the proposed splitting strategy using metrics such as Mean Absolute Error (MAE) and R-squared (R2), and demonstrates its effectiveness in reducing MAE and improving spatial generalization across different cities.

nodes.title: A. Stage 1: spatial clustering for leakage reduction
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a two-stage data splitting procedure for spatial data to reduce leakage and improve spatial generalization in predictive models. The main points covered include the use of Local Moran's I to identify clusters, a two-stage procedure involving spatial clustering and context-aware refinement, and the application of a Spatial Error Model to correct for residual spatial dependence. The document also evaluates the performance of the proposed splitting strategy using metrics such as MAE and R2, and demonstrates its effectiveness in reducing overfitting and improving transferability across different cities. Additionally, the use of XGBoost as a base predictor and the implementation of SEM refinement as post-processing are highlighted. The overall goal is to develop a robust and generalizable predictive model that can handle spatially structured data and reduce the impact of spatial autocorrelation on model performance.

nodes.title: B. Stage 2: context-aware refinement within spatial clusters
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a two-stage data splitting procedure for spatial data to reduce leakage and improve spatial generalization in predictive models. The main points covered include the use of Local Moran's I to identify clusters, a two-stage procedure involving spatial clustering and context-aware refinement, and the application of a Spatial Error Model to correct for residual spatial dependence. The document also evaluates the performance of the proposed splitting strategy using metrics such as MAE and R2, and demonstrates its effectiveness in reducing overfitting and improving transferability across different cities. Additionally, the use of XGBoost as a base predictor and the implementation of SEM refinement as post-processing are highlighted. The overall goal is to develop a robust and generalizable predictive model that accounts for spatial autocorrelation and context heterogeneity.

nodes.title: C. Learning model and spatial error correction
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a two-stage data splitting and spatial error correction approach for improving the predictive performance and spatial generalization of a model in the context of wireless planning. The main points covered include the use of Local Moran's I clusters to identify spatial associations, a two-stage procedure for splitting data into training and validation sets, and the application of a Spatial Error Model (SEM) to correct residual spatial dependence. The approach is evaluated using mean absolute error (MAE) and R2 metrics, and the results show that the two-stage splitting and SEM refinement can reduce MAE and improve predictive performance. The document also presents a case study on 5G NR mid-band (3.5 GHz) wireless planning, where the prediction errors are mapped to bandwidth dimensioning and congestion-risk metrics.

structure.title: V. PERFORMANCE EVALUATION
structure.summary: The partial document discusses a two-stage clustering and spatial error correction framework for improving demand estimates in wireless planning. The main points covered include the comparison of clustering techniques, performance results across five Canadian cities, and learning curves for different clustering strategies. The document also presents a case study on 5G NR mid-band, mapping prediction errors to bandwidth dimensioning and congestion-risk metrics, and defines a planning-level spectral-efficiency abstraction and outage-constrained effective spectral efficiency. Additionally, it approximates the bandwidth required to serve a cell and calculates the induced per-cell bandwidth-error magnitude. The overall goal is to reduce systematic mean absolute error and improve absolute demand estimates for downstream planning.

The document also presents a case study on 5G NR mid-band (3.5 GHz) wireless planning, mapping prediction errors to bandwidth dimensioning and congestion-risk metrics.

structure.title: VI. WIRELESS PLANNING LINK FOR 5G/6G

The main points covered include the comparison of clustering techniques, performance results across five Canadian cities, and learning curves for different clustering strategies. The document also presents a case study on 5G NR mid-band, mapping prediction errors to bandwidth dimensioning and congestion-risk metrics, and defines a planning-level spectral-efficiency abstraction and outage-constrained effective spectral efficiency. nodes.title: A. Bandwidth dimensioning
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses a framework for spatial cellular traffic-demand prediction to support data-driven 5G/6G capacity and spectrum planning. It presents a two-stage clustering and spatial error correction framework that addresses spatial autocorrelation and reduces leakage while preserving functional representativeness. The document evaluates the framework across five major Canadian cities, showing consistent mean absolute error (MAE) reductions relative to location-only clustering, with additional gains from spatial error correction. A 5G NR mid-band case study demonstrates how predictive gains translate into more reliable carrier bandwidth selection and provisioning assessments. The document also explores the impact of prediction errors on bandwidth dimensioning and congestion-risk metrics, highlighting the importance of accurate demand prediction for wireless planning.

nodes.title: B. Congestion risk versus candidate carrier bandwidth
nodes.summary: The partial document discusses an AI-driven framework for spatial cellular traffic-demand prediction to support data-driven 5G/6G capacity and spectrum planning. It addresses spatial autocorrelation through a context-aware two-stage splitting strategy and SEM refinement, which reduces leakage and preserves functional representativeness. The document presents a case study on bandwidth dimensioning error and congestion risk versus candidate carrier bandwidth, highlighting the framework's ability to provide more reliable carrier bandwidth selection and provisioning assessments. It also references various studies and methodologies related to spectrum management and geospatial artificial intelligence. The main points covered include the presentation of a sensitivity table, a case study on "All Cities" bandwidth dimensioning error, and a discussion on congestion risk and feasibility screening of candidate carrier bandwidths.

structure.title: VII. CONCLUSION
structure.summary: The partial document discusses an AI-driven framework for spatial cellular traffic-demand prediction to support data-driven 5G/6G capacity and spectrum planning. It addresses spatial autocorrelation through a context-aware two-stage splitting strategy and SEM refinement, which mitigates residual spatial errors. The document presents a case study on bandwidth dimensioning error and congestion risk versus candidate carrier bandwidth, highlighting the framework's ability to provide more reliable carrier bandwidth selection and provisioning assessments. It also references various studies and methodologies related to spectrum management and geospatial artificial intelligence, demonstrating the framework's potential for improving spectrum planning and management.
